coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis and preparation of graphite/chitosan/graphene quantum dots nanocomposite cathode for electrochemical removal of tetracycline from aqueous solution
coredata.dc:creator: Ebratkhahan, Masoud
Zarei, Mahmoud
Arsalani, Nasser
coredata.dc:description: In this research, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was synthesized via the facile pyrolysis method. The properties of the prepared GQDs were examined by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), dynamic light scattering (DLS), photoluminescence (PL), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) methods. Afterward, the graphite/chitosan/graphene quantum dots (Gr/CS/GQDs) nanocomposite was prepared and used as a cathode electrode in electro-Fenton (EF) process to study the tetracycline (TCL) removal from the aqueous solution. The effect of operational parameters in EF, including current density (mA/cm2), initial pH, initial TCL concentration (mg/L), and process time (min) on the TCL removal efficiency, was investigated via response surface methodology (RSM). The results showed that the optimal values for current density, pH, initial TCL concentration, and process time were 60 mA/cm2, 5, 30 mg/L, and 150 min, respectively. Maximum TCL removal efficiency was 92.98%. FT-IR, XRD, Raman spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy(AFM), SEM, mapping, energy dispersive X-Ray (EDX), Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET), and cyclic voltammetry (CV) analyses were performed to investigate the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode. Finally, gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC-MS), total organic carbon(TOC), and atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) analyses were performed and the reusability of the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode was investigated.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was synthesized via the facile pyrolysis method. The properties of the prepared GQDs were examined by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), dynamic light scattering (DLS), photoluminescence (PL), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) methods. Afterward, the graphite/chitosan/graphene quantum dots (Gr/CS/GQDs) nanocomposite was prepared and used as a cathode electrode in electro-Fenton (EF) process to study the tetracycline (TCL) removal from the aqueous solution. The effect of operational parameters in EF, including current density (mA/cm2), initial pH, initial TCL concentration (mg/L), and process time (min) on the TCL removal efficiency, was investigated via response surface methodology (RSM). The results showed that the optimal values for current density, pH, initial TCL concentration, and process time were 60 mA/cm2, 5, 30 mg/L, and 150 min, respectively. Maximum TCL removal efficiency was 92.98%. FT-IR, XRD, Raman spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy(AFM), SEM, mapping, energy dispersive X-Ray (EDX), Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET), and cyclic voltammetry (CV) analyses were performed to investigate the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode. Finally, gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC-MS), total organic carbon(TOC), and atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) analyses were performed and the reusability of the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode was investigated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is a scarce resource for all living creatures and is the basis of sustainable development. In recent years, environmental pollution and especially water resources pollution has been one of the main concern subjects around the world due to the rapid population growth, excessive water extraction for agricultural irrigation, the significant increase in industrial activities, and lack of purified water resources. Various organic materials such as aromatics, hormones, pesticides, dyes, and pharmaceuticals can enter the water and cause toxicity and carcinogenicity [1–5]. Therefore, it is very crucial to develop effective technologies for the removal of organic pollutants from water.

Over past decades, various advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) methods have been investigated and applied for water and wastewater treatment processes, including Fenton [6], photo-Fenton [7], photocatalytic processes [8,9], sonocatalytic processes [10,11], wet air oxidation [12], anodic oxidation [13], and electro-Fenton (EF) [14,15]. •OH radical has the primary role in AOPs processes for removing organic pollutants and decontaminating of water and wastewater [16]. AOPs based on electrical current and electrochemical reactions are called electrochemical advanced oxidation processes (EAOPs). EAOPs are based on the in-situ production of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in solution via electrical current. The main advantages of EAOPs are easy handling, high process efficiency, and environmental compatibility. EF is one of the most common examples of the EAOPs methods for wastewater treatment. The in-situ production of H2O2 in the EF process reduces the transportation and storage costs of the H2O2, increases the process safety, and helps the process to be easily carried out at ambient pressure and temperature [17]. The following reactions occur in the EF process (Eqs. 1–3) [18]:

Pharmaceuticals are a group of chemicals used in large quantities to cure various diseases and ensure a healthier and longer life. Pharmaceuticals enter the environment through industrial, domestic, and urban sources. They are considered a significant threat to humans and other organisms due to their high persistence to degradation, long retention time, and their effect on the genetics of microorganisms. Among the various pharmaceuticals, antibiotics have received considerable attention due to their widespread use [19,20]. Tetracycline (TCL) is a group of antibiotics widely used for human and veterinary purposes due to its broad spectrum of activity, oral availability, and low cost. It is present in surface water and groundwater resources with a concentration range of 0.22 to 10000 ng/L [21,22]. Its increased concentration in water may cause gastrointestinal irritation, vomiting, diarrhea, and renal failure [23]. TCL is a non-biodegradable, low volatile, highly hydrophilic, with a relatively long half-life (up to 180 days) [24,25] which makes it of high priority to remove from aqueous media.

Carbon-based nanomaterials such as graphene sheets, carbon nanotubes, carbon quantum dots, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) cab be very helpful in various fields such as environmental sensing, health, and environmental clean-up due to their unique properties [26–28]. Graphene is defined as a single layer of carbon atoms. The main properties of graphene are high specific surface area, high porosity, high chemical stability, and excellent electron transport [29].

GQDs, as a new type of zero-dimensional quantum dot, have attracted much attention in nanomaterials technology due to their unique properties, such as low toxicity, controllable size, chemical stability, high thermal conductivity, wavelength tenability, and high optical and catalytic properties [30–32]. GQDs have great potential for applications such as anticancer therapy, biosensors, photocatalysis, luminescence, and electrochemistry [33–37]. Overall, GQDs can be synthesized via two methods: the top-down method and the bottom-up method. The top-down approach cuts sp2-dominated carbon materials such as graphene sheets, carbon nanotubes, and graphite (Gr) into small sizes through different methods like hydrothermal, electrochemical oxidation, and acidic exfoliation. The bottom-up approach refers to the preparation of GQDs from small molecules like citric acid (CA), glucose, adenosine triphosphate, and benzene derivatives via solution chemistry, carbonizing particular organic precursors, and fragmentation of suitable precursor methods [38]. The average particle size of GQDs is below 10 nm [39].

In this research, the novel graphite/chitosan/graphene quantum dots (Gr/CS/GQDs) composite electrode was prepared for TCL removal via the EF process. GQDs were synthesized via a simple pyrolysis method. Characterization analyses for GQDs, including high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), dynamic light scattering (DLS), photoluminescence (PL), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) were performed. Then, FT-IR, XRD, Raman spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy(AFM), SEM, mapping, energy dispersive X-Ray (EDX), Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET), and cyclic voltammetry (CV) analyses were performed to confirm the successful synthesis of the composites and to investigate the Gr/CS/GQDs electrode. Then, the factors that affect the TCL removal were evaluated via response surface methodology (RSM). The investigated parameters are applied current density (mA/cm2), initial pH, initial TCL concentration (mg/L), and process time (min). Finally, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS), total organic carbon(TOC), and atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) analyses were performed and the reusability performance of the Gr/CS/GQDs electrode was investigated.

Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥ 97.0%), ethanol (99%), iron (II) sulfate (FeSO4, 88.7%), CA (C6H8O7, 99.5%), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, 99%), n-butanol (C4H10O, ≥ 99.5%), p-benzoquinone (C6H4O2, ≥ 98%), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3 ≥ 99.5%), potassium iodide (KI, ≥ 99%), ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24, ≥ 99%), potassium hydrogen phthalate (C8H5KO4, ≥ 99.5%), dichloromethane (CH2Cl2, ≥ 99.8%), 1-Methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP, C5H9NO, ≥ 99.5%), and N,O-bis-(trimethylsilyl) acetamide (C8H21NOSi2, ≥ 95%,) were purchased from Merck® (Germany) and used as received. Gr powder was supplied from Dae jung® (South Korea). Chitosan (CS, medium molecular weight) was bought from Titrachem® (Iran). Polytetrafluoroethylene ((C2F4)n, PTFE) was bought from Kaisa Polymer (Iran). TCL (Table 1 ) was purchased from Iran Daru pharmacy Co (Iran).

BMG magnetic stirrer (Iran), 86502AZ pH meter (Taiwan), and Adak PS-808 DC power supply (Iran) were applied in this research work. Shimadzu four-digit digital scale (Japan) was used for accurately weigh the solid materials. Specord 250, Analytic Jena, UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Germany) was used for UV–Vis analysis and measurement. The functional groups of the materials were studied via a Tensor 27, Bruker (Germany) FT-IR instrument. Photoluminescence (PL) analysis was carried out via PerkinElmer LS45 (United States) device. Microtrac Flex, Nanotrac wave model (United States) was used for dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis. HRTEMimages of GQDs were taken by HRTEM FEI TECNAI F20 (United States) device. The crystallinity of the materials was investigated by a Tongda TD-3700 X-Ray diffractometer (China). Raman analysis was performed via Andor DR316B_LD (United Kingdom) instrument at 532 nm. Surface roughness of the electrode was carried out via the Nanosurf Mobile S AFM instrument (Switzerland). Surface studies for BET analysis were done with Belsorp mini II, Microtrac Bel Corp (Japan), with N2 gas flow. Potentiostat/Galvanostat, Autolab/PGSTAT 100 (the Netherlands) instrument was used for recording the CV plot. The surface morphology of the electrode was investigated via MIRA3 TESCAN SEM instrument (Czech Republic). For TOC measurement of the samples, Shimadzu TOC device model TOC-VCHS (Japan) was used. For the AAS analysis of the iron, the special iron hollow cathode lamp at the wavelength 248.3 nm was used. The sample was entered into the AAS instrument (Analytic Jena, Nova 400, Germany) without further preparation. 10 mL of the optimum sample according to RSM results was given to AAS analysis. GC–MS analysis was done via an Agilent 6890 gas chromatography coupled with an Agilent 5973 mass spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, Palo Alto, Canada). The GC-MS sample preparation technique and temperature programming were performed according to our recent work [40]. 10 mL of the prepared optimum aqueous sample, according to RSM experiment results, was transferred to 10 mL of the dicholoromethane as the organic phase. The extraction was repeated three times. Then, the sample was evaporated and silylated with 100 μL of N,O-bis trimethylsilyl. The applied temperature programming was: starting at 50 °C for 40 min, increasing the temperature 8 °C/min until 300 °C, and keeping 300 °C for 4 min.

GQD particles were synthesized via pyrolysis, an easy bottom-up method. 2 g of CA was poured into a 50 mL beaker and heated up to 200 °C on a heating mantle for about 30 min until an orange liquid was obtained. The CA started to liquidate after 5 min of heating and the liquid's color changed from colorless to yellow and finally to orange. Then, the obtained orange liquid was added dropwise into 100 mL of 10 mg/mL NaOH solution, under vigorous stirring, and was neutralized to pH = 7. The obtained GQDs were kept in the dark at 4 °C to further use. To obtain solid GQD particles, vacuum freeze-drying was used [41,42].

In the current project, the gas diffusion electrode (GDE) was coupled with the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode due to the excellent efficiency of GDE in H2O2 production. To prepare Gr/CS/GQDs nanocomposite electrode, 0.1 g of Gr, 0.1 g CS, and 0.1 g GQDs were mixed with 0.4 g of 6% PTFE, 16 mL of NMP, and 1.5 mL of n-butanol. The GQDs are highly soluble in the aqueous media and this property of GQDs can be helpful in various fields; nevertheless, their high solubility in aqueous media limits their direct use in preparing modified electrodes. Since GQDs leaking from the electrode into the solution is possible, CS as a natural polymer can help to overcome this problem and increase the stability of the GQDs containing electrodes. CS is a weak alkaline soluble in dilute aqueous acetic acid solution. It has R-NH2 units, which can be protonated to R-NH2 + in the alkaline medium [29,43]. In this experiment, it is expected that CS can help to keep GQD in the mixture via the strong electrostatic interaction between the positively charged CS and negatively charged GQD.

The mixture was dispersed via an ultrasonic probe for 15 min to obtain a uniform solution. Next, the dispersed solution was heated and evaporated until the dough-like material was obtained. This obtained material was brushed onto a circular stainless steel mesh with a 25 mm diameter to obtain a thin layer. Stainless steel was washed with ethanol and distilled water twice to clean the surface before the brushing stage. The obtained layer was pressed under 5 Mbar to obtain a uniform and smooth surface of the Gr/CS/GQDs thin layer. The obtained layer was put in the furnace at 350 °C for 30 min under the inert N2 gas flow to calcine. The obtained Gr/CS/GQDs electrode was placed into the end of cylindrical GDE, with a graphite ring behind the Gr/CS/GQDs. The graphite ring was attached to copper wire to transmit the electrical current. Finally, the fabricated Gr/CS/GQDs electrode was tested to inspect its ability to remove TCL from an aqueous medium.

The TCL removing experiments were carried out in a 250 mL beaker containing 100 mL of TCL solution. A magnetic stirrer for stirring the solutions, a DC power supply for applying the current, and an air pump for generating the needed O2 of the reaction were used. The cathode electrode was Gr/CS/GQDs nanocomposite with a circular shape, 25 mm diameter, and thickness of 0.5 mm. Pt electrode with dimensions of 3 cm × 3 cm was used as the anode. The distance between the cathode and anode was 1 cm for all experiments. 0.05 mol/L sodium sulfate solution was added as the electrolyte to accelerate charge transfer [44]. Iron (II) sulfate 1 mmol/L was added to the reaction medium to supply the required iron for the EF reaction. To adjust the solution's pH, sulfuric acid 0.1 mol/L and sodium hydroxide 0.1 mol/L solutions were added when needed. According to the proposed program of experimental design software (Design Expert 12), 31 samples were prepared. To determine the removal efficiency (RE%) of the EF process, the sampling was performed at specific times. The absorbance of the tested samples was measured via spectrophotometric methodology.

In the present research, the central composition design (CCD) based RSM was selected for TCL removal modeling. The effect of four main factors affecting the TCL removal, including the current density (mA/cm2), the initial pH, initial TCL concentration (mg/L), and reaction time (min), was investigated. The experiments were designed via RSM based on the four factors mentioned. The parameters range and levels are specified in Table 2 . The second-order equation (Eq. 4) of the following polynomials was used to correlate the dependent and non-dependent variables.
(4)

where Y is the response variable (removal efficiency), Xi and Xj are the experimental levels of the variables (TCL concentration (mg/L), pH, current density (mA/cm2), Time (min)), βi is the correlation coefficient of linear factors, βii is the correlation coefficient of quadratic factors, βij is the correlation coefficient of factors multiplied by each other and i, j, and k is the number of variables [45].

Fig. 1 a shows the HRTEM image of the prepared GQDs. The presence of GQDs with an average particle size of about 1–2 nm can be easily observed in this image. D fringe space of 0.345 nm is attributed to the 2θ = 26° and (002) planes. D fringe space of 0.239 nm is attributed to the 2θ = 37.5° and (1120) planes and, in some cases, (100) planes. Fig. 1b depicts the SEM image of the prepared GQDs. The surface morphology of GQDs displays numerous cavities and severe wrinkles on the surface. Fig. 1c demonstrates the XRD pattern of the synthesized GQDs. There isn't any sharp peak at the GQDs XRD pattern. This is attributed to the amorphous and disordered structure of the GQDs, which is due to the presence of oxygenated functional groups in the GQDs structure. There is only a broad peak at 2θ = 26°, which can be ascribed to the (002) planes of the hexagonal graphitic structure of the GQDs with a 0.345 nm distance between the planes, proposing that carbonizing of the CA has led to producing graphitic structure.

Fig. 2 a demonstrates the DLS results of the GQDs. The average particle size of GQDs varies from 0.80 nm to 1.90 nm, which is in accordance with HRTEM results. Most (38.05%) GQDs particle sizes are about 0.95 nm confirming the nano size structure of GQDs. Fig. 2b illustrates the PL results of the GQDs. PL can be used as one of the evident analyses to prove the presence of GQDs. The maximum excitation of GQDs was observed at a wavelength of 325 nm and the maximum emission of GQDs was observed at a wavelength of 432 nm. The results claim the successful synthesis of GQDs from CA. Fig. 2c depicts the FT-IR results of the GQDs. Three obvious peaks were observed for GQDs. At wavenumber of 3428 cm−1, stretching vibration peak of O–H functional groups was observed. The peaks at 1580 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of the C=C bond [41]. The peak at 1391 cm-1 can be ascribed to the flexure vibration of C–OH and demonstrates that the GQDs were rich in oxygenated functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl) [46]. Fig. 2d displays the UV–Vis results of the GQDs. At the wavelength of 356 nm, the UV absorption peak of GQDs was observed and under UV light irradiation, it can be seen the synthesized GQDs are blue luminescent (Fig. 2d inset).

To investigate the functional groups of the Gr/CS/GQDs nanocomposite, FT-IR analysis was performed. Fig. 3 a illustrates the FT-IR spectra of the Gr/CS/GQDs nanocomposite. Due to the abundance of the C, O, and H elements in the structure of the Gr/CS/GQDs nanocomposite, it is more probable to see peaks that consisting the vibration of the mentioned elements. The peak of the O–H functional groups of hydroxyl and carboxyl was observed at 3276 cm−1. The peaks at 2921 and 2825 cm−1 corresponded to C–H bonds of Gr/CS/GQDs nanocomposite. The stretching vibration peak of the C=O bond was detected at 1671. The peak at 1569 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the C=C bond. In 1410 cm-1, a peak related to C-O-H bond bending vibration was observed [29].

Fig. 3b illustrates the XRD results of the Gr/CS/GQDs nanocomposite. The crystallographic composition of the Gr/CS/GQDs nanocomposite was compared by the Joint Committee of Powder Diffraction Data Standards (JCPDS) cards. GQDs, CS(JCPDS no 039-1894), and Gr(JCPDS no 41–1487) peaks were observed in the Gr/CS/GQDs nanocomposite structure. CS peak was observed at 2θ = 17.93° with 274 planes [47]. The sharp peak at 2θ = 26.54° was attributed to 002 planes of Gr and GQDs with the intercellular spacing of 0.345 nm [48]. Moreover, the peaks of 39.24° at 100 plane, 44.08° at 101 plane, 56.24° at 004 plane, and 70.56° at 112 plane signify the neat and layered structure of Gr [49,50]. These results confirm the presence of mentioned materials at the ordered structure of the Gr/CS/GQDs nanocomposite.

Microstructures of the synthesized Gr/CS/GQDs were studied via Raman spectroscopy. Fig. 3c illustrates the Raman spectra of the Gr/CS/GQDs nanocomposite. The D-band and G-band peaks of the Gr/CS/GQDs nanocomposite were observed at 1334 and 1569 cm−1 [51,52]. The D-band displays the conversion of sp2-to sp3-hybridized carbon, while the G band represents sp2 carbon atoms in-plane vibration. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) can be used to study structural defects and disorders. This amount for the Gr/CS/GQDs nanocomposite was about 1.09. The presence of the D band in the Raman spectrum and the amount of ID/IG ratio show that some of the functional groups remain in the structure of the GQDs, which creates defects in the structure [53,54].

To examine the topography of the Gr/CS/GQDs electrode, AFM analysis was performed. Fig. 4 a shows a three-dimensional (3D) image of the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode surface. In this image, light-colored areas the peaks and dark-colored areas are the valleys of the examined Gr/CS/GQDs cathode surface. Increasing the pores on the surface of the electrode increases the surface area and the amount of oxygen penetration to the surface of the electrode. This will increase H2O2 production. Surface roughness parameters analysis results are presented in Table 3 . The values, including Sa, Sq, Sy, Sp, Sv, and Sm refer to mean roughness, secondary root of mean roughness, peak-valley height, peak height, valley depth, and mean value of the cathode’s surface, respectively. The AFM results claim high surface roughness and cavities depth on the surface of the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode, resulting in having more available surface area. The difference between the Sp and Sv values for Gr/CS/GQDs cathode is 282 nm. Higher surface area leads to more electron transfer rate at the cathode and enhances the EF reaction kinetic, which is favorable for higher TCL removal efficiency via the EF process.

Fig. 4b-d display SEM, mapping, and EDX results of Gr/CS/GQDs cathode. As shown in Fig. 4b, the SEM image of the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode shows the mixture of Gr/CS/GQDs nanocomposite cathode has a homogenous structure. The structure indicates most of the CS nanoparticles are placed uniformly with different sizes on the graphitic layers, as it is hard to distinguish GQDs nanoparticles via SEM analysis. The mean size of the prepared composite particles was about 200 nm. The mapping results in Fig. 4c claim the presence of C, O, N, and Fe elements on the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode's surface. The weight percentage of C, O, N, and Fe elements on the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode's surface, as presented in Fig. 4d is 71.55%, 18.75%, 5.68%, and 4.02%, respectively.

Moreover, the BET analysis was performed for Gr/CS/GQDs to study the surface properties. The BET result for 0.0753 mg of Gr/CS/GQDs depicts BET surface area of Gr/CS/GQDs was 3.6062 m2/g. The pore volume of Gr/CS/GQDs was 0.8285 cm3/g. The mean pore diameter of Gr/CS/GQDs was 13.897 nm. Fig. 5 displays the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm of Gr/CS/GQDs cathode. IV-type adsorption–desorption isotherm with an H3-type hysteresis loop was observed for Gr/CS/GQDs cathode. This indicates that Gr/CS/GQDs cathode has a mesoporous structure, which is favorable for EF reactions.

The electrochemical behavior of the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode was investigated via CV analysis. The CV curve of the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode was plotted in 0.05 mol/L Na2SO4 solution, at pH = 5, in the presence of air and a scan speed of 50 mV/s. CV experiment was performed in a three-electrode system in which Gr/CS/GQDs, Pt (with dimensions of 3 cm × 3 cm), and standard Ag/AgCl electrodes were the working, auxiliary, and reference electrodes of the CV analysis, respectively. Fig. 6 depicts the CV results of the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode from −1 V to 1 V. As can be seen, Gr/CS/GQDs cathode demonstrated a reduction peak at −0.3 V, which confirms the results of BET analysis about the high surface area of the Gr/CS/GQDs. It can be concluded that at Gr/CS/GQDs, the amount of oxygen penetration on the surface of the electrode is increased and sufficient surface is available for reduction reactions. Therefore, the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode's reduction peak can be attributed to the high effective surface area of the cathode.

The experiments were performed as displayed in Table 4 . Based on the results, Eq. 5 was obtained via software among the response variable and the independent variables in an uncoded form.
(5) Removal efficiency (%) = 77.98915 + 0.373697 A – 1.33319B + 0.200915C– 0.215806 D – 0.003154A2 – 0.054483 B2 – 0.013442C2 + 0.002144 D2 + 0.024203AB + 0.005447AC –0.001184 AD – 0.004406 BC –0.012135 BD + 0.001444CD.

Herein, the response variable is the removal efficiency of TCL, and A, B, C, and D are the four investigated variables. Table 4 displays the experimental results and the predicted results as a function of obtained Eq. 5. Comparing the experimental and predicted results of RSM, it can be seen that the accuracy of the resulting model is high and acceptable. To evaluate the correctness and significance of the attained model, analysis of variance (ANOVA) is usually applied. The obtained correlation coefficient value (R2 = 96.72%) is appropriate and standard, as depicted in Fig. 7 . It shows that 96.72% of the answer can be justified, and only 3.24% of the results can’t explain by the model. The high F value (33.72) and P-value of < 0.0001 (Table 5 ) also prove the high significance of the model. A small coefficient of variation (CV = 1.84%) and the value of the standard deviation (SD = 1.49%) validated the experiment's good accuracy, reproducibility, and reliability. The non-significance of lack of fit (P-value = 0.1623) indicated how well this model can interpret and predict removal efficiency. P-values<0.05 indicate model terms are significant. Therefore, A, B, C, D, AB, AC, A2, C2, and D2 are significant model terms (Eq. 6):
(6) Removal efficiency (%) = 77.98915 + 0.373697 A – 1.33319B + 0.200915C– 0.215806 D – 0.003154A2 – 0.013442C2 + 0.002144 D2 + 0.024203AB + 0.005447AC.

The impact of each variable and their interaction on TCL removal efficiency can be examined via Pareto analysis. The related equations for this analysis are the obtained coefficients in Eq. (2) and the calculations via Eq. (7).

where P i is the effect of each parameter and the b i is the coefficient of each parameter [55]. Fig. 8 displays each variable’s impact on the response variable. According to this, pH, current density, reaction time, and initial TCL concentration have the most impact on the EF process by 6.11, 1.71, 0.99, and 0.92%, respectively. Among the remained variables, pH × pH also has an impact of 0.25%. The effect of the other variables and their interaction is lower than the mentioned variables and can be neglected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variables effect on TCL removal efficiency
Effect of initial TCL concentration and current density on TCL removal efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 a depicts 2D and 3D graphs of the TCL removal efficiency as a function of initial TCL concentration and current density for a solution with an initial pH = 5 and reaction time = 90 min. According to Fig. 9a, by increasing the initial TCL concentration from 10 to 50 mg/L, the removal efficiency remains constant after 90 min of the experimentation until 30 mg/L. It then decreases gradually to 50 mg/L. At lower concentrations, the reaction rate of TCL molecules on the surface of the electrode is high enough, leading to a competition between TCL molecules to react with in-situ produced ●OH radicals. These intermediates are responsible for the removal of the pollutant. While the concentration of pollutant molecules increases, there won’t be enough ●OH radicals for the complete removal of TCL. As a result, the TCL removal efficiency will be reduced. At lower concentrations (10 to 30 mg/L), the TCL removal efficiency will be the highest. At higher concentrations, due to the higher rate of contaminants accumulation and the production of the intermediates, which are potentially competing for ●OH radicals and active sites, the TCL removal efficiency will be reduced [56,57].

Fig. 9a displays that by increasing the current density, the TCL removal efficiency first increases gradually and then remains constant. The current density is one of the most critical parameters in the EF process efficiency since it affects the production of ●OH radicals. Generally, increasing the current density will accelerate the H2O2 formation in the cathode and as a result, more ●OH radicals will be produced [58]. As shown in Fig. 9a, the TCL removal efficiency increases by increasing the applied current density to 60 mA/cm2. In this range, increasing the applied current may result in increased H2O2 production. Therefore, a larger number of ●OH radicals being generated. With a further increase in the intensity of the electric current, the removal efficiency increases slowly and finally remains constant. At higher current densities, possible side reactions such as the O2 and H2 release at the reaction medium reduce H2O2 production and the concentration of ●OH radicals. This will result in a decrease in the TCL removal efficiency. In addition, higher current densities will increase the operational cost of the EF process and some of the applied current density will also be used to convert electrical energy into thermal energy, which is not favorable [59,60]. Therefore, 60 mA/cm2 was selected as the optimum current density of the EF process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of initial TCL concentration and pH on TCL removal efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9b depicts 2D and 3D graphs of TCL removal efficiency as a function of initial TCL concentration and pH for a solution with a current density = 60 mA/cm2 and reaction time = 90 min. By increasing the initial pH, the TCL removal efficiency increases until the pH = 5. The best pH range for TCL removal is 3 < pH < 5. Then, the TCL removal efficiency decreases at higher pHs. One reason is the formation of Fe(OH)3 precipitate, which causes H2O2 to decompose into O2 and H2O, and leads to a reduction in the removal of TCL. The decrease of TCL removal efficiency on basic pH may be due to reduced water oxidation and thus reduced production of ●OH radicals. Since the oxidizing power of the ●OH radicals is higher at low pHs and one of the main reactions of the EF process is the production of ●OH radicals; therefore, lower pHs will be more efficient [61,62]. Considering the mentioned subjects, the initial pH = 5 was selected as the optimal pH for succeeding experiments since less chemical amount will be needed to neutralize the effluent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of initial TCL concentration and reaction time on TCL removal efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9c demonstrates 2D and 3D graphs of TCL removal efficiency as a function of initial TCL concentration and reaction time for a solution with a current density = 60 mA/cm2 and initial pH = 5. According to Fig. 9c, the TCL removal efficiency was enhanced when prolonging EF process time. Generally, most TCL removal happens in the first 30 min of the process with a quick reaction rate among the TCL molecules and ●OH radicals. Then, the TCL removal efficiency slope reduces over time and finally remains at a constant amount since the amount of active ●OH radicals is reduced. It can be concluded increasing the reaction time results in more opportunities for ●OH radicals to attack and enhance the TCL removal efficiency over time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determining the optimal TCL removal conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Accomplishing the best viable pharmaceutical removal efficiency is one of the fundamental aims of the EF methods. The optimal TCL removal conditions were determined via investigating the variables which are effective at TCL removal. Considering this point, the optimal test conditions for each variable are stated in Table 6 . The maximum achieved TCL removal efficiency was 92.98% at current density = 60 mA/cm2, initial pH = 5, initial TCL concentration = 30 mg/L, and process time = 150 min. The obtained test results revealed the correlation between the experimental value (92.98%) and the predicted value (93.97%) is high, which claims the achievement of the proposed RSM model in optimizing the TCL removal through the EF process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scavengers effect and H2O2 production investigation
ce:section.ce:para:
The AOPs-based processes' efficiency highly depends on the ●OH radical generation since it has an essential role in organic compounds removal via the EF process. The ●OH scavenging effect was inspected on TCL removal by adding 10 mL of p-benzoquinone (BQ), ethanol (EtOH) as organic scavengers and sodium carbonate (CO3 2–) as the inorganic scavenger. It must be noted the aforementioned scavengers were added separately to the TCL-containing solution. The concentration of each added scavenger was 10 mmol/L. Fig. 10 a displays the scavengers’ effect on TCL removal efficiency. As expected, the presence of EtOH, CO3 2–, and BQ scavengers resulted in a reduction in TCL removal efficiency from 92.98% to 83.07, 80.24, and 75.80%, respectively. Among the applied scavengers, BQ has reduced TCL removal efficiency more than the other scavengers. EtOH and CO3 2– are suppressing ●OH, while BQ is suppressing O2 ●- during the EF process, according to Eqs. 8–10 [63,64]:

Fig. 10b depicts the H2O2 generation of Gr/CS/GQDs cathode over 150 min. It was observed the amount of electro-generated H2O2 was 1372 µmol/L. The H2O2 yield on the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode is mainly due to the porous structure of the Gr/CS/GQD s cathode, which accelerates the O2 diffusion on the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode’s surface and provides sufficient O2 for the H2O2 generation. This also accelerated the rapid transfer of H2O2 to the solution, preventing the further conversion of H2O2 into H2O. The obtained results are in good accordance with previous research [65].

TOC analysis was performed at optimum points (current density = 60 mA/cm2, initial pH = 5, and initial TCL concentration = 30 mg/L) of the variables. The results confirmed the TOC amount of TCL-containing solution was reduced after 6 h of reaction time, and the mineralization efficiency finally reached 80.45%. This confirms the TCL has been effectively removed from the aqueous environment via the EF process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AAS analysis result and cathode reusability check
ce:section.ce:para: The AAS results showed that the concentration of the leached Fe in the aqueous solution was 190.40 µg/L at the end of the EF process, which is acceptable and lower than the permissible limit of Fe in drinking water according to the literature [66].

For investigating the stability of the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode, reusability tests were implemented in eight successive series at optimal experimental conditions (current density = 60 mA/cm2, initial pH = 5, initial TCL concentration = 30 mg/L, and process time = 150 min). The obtained results in Fig. 12 illustrate that the TCL removal efficiency of the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode after eight cycles was reduced by only 4.34%, from 92.98% in the first run to 88.64% in the eighth run. The results specify that the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode has an excellent reusability performance at removing the TCL from the aqueous environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Literature comparison and real wastewater check
ce:section.ce:para: Table 8 shows the comparison table of similar EF works for TCL removal. The EF method via Gr/CS/GQDs cathode has excellent removal efficiency for organic compounds compared to the similar literature for TCL removal. This obtained result confirms the high potential of Gr/CS/GQDs cathode for eliminating organic compounds from an aqueous solution.

To investigate the effectiveness of the EF process in the TCL removal, a real wastewater sample including 30 mg/L TCL was obtained from Tabriz city in Iran and tested. The characteristic of the real wastewater is given in Table 9 . After 150 min of EF process at current density = 60 mA/cm2 and adjusted pH = 5, 86.24% removal of TCL in wastewater was achieved. The lower TCL removal efficiency of the real sample compared to the synthetic one (6.74%) was due to the complex nature of real wastewater with competing constituents with TCL.

In the present study, a facile method was used to synthesize GQDs and fabricate Gr/CS/GQDs cathode. The GQDs synthesis was confirmed via HRTEM, SEM, XRD, DLS, FT-IR, PL, and UV–Vis analyses and the resulting Gr/CS/GQDs cathode was characterized through FT-IR, XRD, Raman, AFM, SEM, mapping, EDX, BET, and CV methods. The results confirm the successful synthesis of the Gr/CS/GQDs cathode for TCL removal at EF system. To ascertain the effects of the operational parameters, the RSM analysis was completed and optimum conditions of the EF process were determined. The optimum removal circumstances are current density = 60 mA/cm2, initial pH = 5, initial TCL concentration = 30 mg/L, and process time = 90 min. The obtained results revealed pH, current density, reaction time, and initial TCL concentration have the most effect on the EF process by 6.11, 1.71, 0.99, and 0.92 %, respectively. The Gr/CS/GQDs cathode showed the highest TCL removal efficiency of 92.98% under optimum conditions. Ten compounds were identified via the GC-MS procedure and the results showed no toxic intermediates. The TOC results demonstrate that about 80.45% of the TCL mineralization has been done. AAS results showed very low amount of the leached Fe at the end of the EF process. The results depict Gr/CS/GQDs possessing outstanding reusability after eight runs and the TCL removal efficiency was only decreased by 4.34%. The results proved that the EF method with Gr/CS/GQDs cathode is a convenient and cost-effective method for removing TCL from the aqueous medium. This EAOP process can be utilized to remove various types of pharmaceutical contaminants from aqueous environs.
Masoud Ebratkhahan: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Mahmoud Zarei: Supervision. Nasser Arsalani: Visualization.
